en.extract:
The rusty-fronted canastero (Asthenes ottonis) is a species of bird in the Furnariinae subfamily of the ovenbird family Furnariidae. It is endemic to Peru.


Taxonomy and systematics
The rusty-fronted canastero is monotypic. At times it has variously been treated as a subspecies of the canyon canastero (A. pudibunda) and as a superspecies with it and the maquis canastero (A. heterura), but genetic data show it to be most closely related to the eye-ringed thistletail (A. palpebralis).


Description
The rusty-fronted canastero is 18 to 19 cm (7.1 to 7.5 in) long and weighs about 13 g (0.46 oz). The sexes have the same plumage. Adults have a grayish buff supercilium and arc below the eye, a small dark line in front of the eye, and a dark brownish patch behind the eye on an otherwise dull brownish face with pale streaks. Their forehead ("front") is rufous with faint pale streaks. Their crown, back, and rump are rich brown and their uppertail coverts chestnut-rufous. Their wings are mostly rufous-chestnut with dark fuscous tips on the flight feathers. Their tail is rufous-chestnut; the inner three pairs of feathers are longer than the others and have dark fuscous shafts. Their chin and upper throat are light orange-rufous to chestnut-rufous, their lower throat pale grayish buff streaked with brownish gray, their breast slightly darker and streaked with pale buff, their belly buff-whitish, and their flanks and undertail coverts rufescent brown. Their iris is dark brown to brown, their maxilla dark horn to blackish, their mandible silvery to dark horn with a dark tip, and their legs and feet olive-gray to dark gray.


Distribution and habitat
The rusty-fronted canastero is found in the Andes of south-central Peru's Huancavelica, Ayacucho, Apurímac, and Cuzco departments. It inhabits semi-humid to arid montane scrublands and the edge of Polylepis woodlands. In elevation it ranges between 2,750 and 4,000 m (9,000 and 13,100 ft).


Behavior


Movement
The rusty-fronted canastero is a year-round resident throughout its range.


Feeding
The rusty-fronted canastero's diet and foraging behavior are not well known. It is usually seen singly or in pairs. It is thought to feed on arthropods gleaned from low vegetation and the ground.


Breeding
Nothing is known about the rusty-fronted canastero's breeding biology.


Vocalization
The rusty-fronted canastero's song is "3 high-pitched ascending notes followed by a rapidly descending series, 'bsee-bzéé-bzee-di-di-di-di-d-d-d' ". Its call has been described as "high-pitched 'veer' and nasal 'djeeh' ".


Status
The IUCN has assessed the rusty-fronted canastero as being of Least Concern. It has a somewhat limited range and an unknown population size that is believed to be stable. No immediate threats have been identified. It is poorly known but thought to be fairly common. It occurs in at least one protected area.


References

The '''rusty-fronted canastero''' ('''''Asthenes ottonis''''') is a species of [[bird]] in the Furnariinae subfamily of the ovenbird [[Family (taxonomy)|family]] [[Furnariidae]]. It is [[Endemism|endemic]] to [[Peru]].<ref name=IOC13.2>{{cite web |url=https://www.worldbirdnames.org/new/bow/ovenbirds/ |title=Ovenbirds, woodcreepers |website=IOC World Bird List |version =v 13.2 | editor1-last=Gill | editor1-first=Frank | editor1-link=Frank Gill (ornithologist) | editor2-last=Donsker | editor2-first=David | editor3-last=Rasmussen | editor3-first=Pamela | editor3-link=Pamela Rasmussen |date=July 2023 |access-date=July 31, 2023 }}</ref>

==Taxonomy and systematics==

The rusty-fronted canastero is [[monotypic]].<ref name=IOC13.2/> At times it has variously been treated as a subspecies of the [[canyon canastero]] (''A. pudibunda'') and as a [[superspecies]] with it and the [[maquis canastero]] (''A. heterura''), but genetic data show it to be most closely related to the [[eye-ringed thistletail]] (''A. palpebralis'').<ref name=SACClist>Remsen, J. V., Jr., J. I. Areta, E. Bonaccorso, S. Claramunt, G. Del-Rio, A. Jaramillo, D. F. Lane, M. B. Robbins, F. G. Stiles, and K. J. Zimmer. Version 28 September 2023. A classification of the bird species of South America. American Ornithological Society. https://www.museum.lsu.edu/~Remsen/SACCBaseline.htm retrieved October 20, 2023</ref><ref name=RFCA-BOW>Remsen, Jr., J. V. (2020). Rusty-fronted Canastero (''Asthenes ottonis''), version 1.0. In Birds of the World (J. del Hoyo, A. Elliott, J. Sargatal, D. A. Christie, and E. de Juana, Editors). Cornell Lab of Ornithology, Ithaca, NY, USA. https://doi.org/10.2173/bow.rufcan1.01 retrieved November 7, 2023</ref>

==Description==

The rusty-fronted canastero is {{convert|18|to|19|cm|in|sigfig=2|abbr=on}} long and weighs about {{convert|13|g|oz|abbr=on}}. The sexes have the same plumage. Adults have a grayish buff [[supercilium]] and arc below the eye, a small dark line in front of the eye, and a dark brownish patch behind the eye on an otherwise dull brownish face with pale streaks. Their forehead ("front") is rufous with faint pale streaks. Their crown, back, and rump are rich brown and their uppertail [[Covert feather|coverts]] chestnut-rufous. Their wings are mostly rufous-chestnut with dark fuscous tips on the flight feathers. Their tail is rufous-chestnut; the inner three pairs of feathers are longer than the others and have dark fuscous shafts. Their chin and upper throat are light orange-rufous to chestnut-rufous, their lower throat pale grayish buff streaked with brownish gray, their breast slightly darker and streaked with pale buff, their belly buff-whitish, and their flanks and undertail coverts rufescent brown. Their iris is dark brown to brown, their [[maxilla]] dark horn to blackish, their [[mandible]] silvery to dark horn with a dark tip, and their legs and feet olive-gray to dark gray.<ref name=RFCA-BOW/>

==Distribution and habitat==

The rusty-fronted canastero is found in the Andes of south-central Peru's [[Department of Huancavelica|Huancavelica]], [[Department of Ayacucho|Ayacucho]], [[Department of Apurímac|Apurímac]], and [[Department of Cuzco|Cuzco]] departments. It inhabits semi-humid to arid montane scrublands and the edge of ''[[Polylepis]]'' woodlands. In elevation it ranges between {{convert|2750|and|4000|m|ft|-2|abbr=on}}.<ref name=RFCA-BOW/>

==Behavior==
===Movement===

The rusty-fronted canastero is a year-round resident throughout its range.<ref name=RFCA-BOW/>

===Feeding===

The rusty-fronted canastero's diet and foraging behavior are not well known. It is usually seen singly or in pairs. It is thought to feed on [[arthropod]]s gleaned from low vegetation and the ground.<ref name=RFCA-BOW/>

===Breeding===

Nothing is known about the rusty-fronted canastero's breeding biology.<ref name=RFCA-BOW/>

{{birdsong|url=https://xeno-canto.org/species/Asthenes-ottonis |species=rusty-fronted canastero}}
===Vocalization===

The rusty-fronted canastero's song is "3 high-pitched ascending notes followed by a rapidly descending series, 'bsee-bzéé-bzee-di-di-di-di-d-d-d' ". Its call has been described as "high-pitched 'veer' and nasal 'djeeh' ".<ref name=RFCA-BOW/>

==Status==

The [[IUCN]] has assessed the rusty-fronted canastero as being of Least Concern. It has a somewhat limited range and an unknown population size that is believed to be stable. No immediate threats have been identified.<ref name=IUCN/> It is poorly known but thought to be fairly common. It occurs in at least one protected area.<ref name=RFCA-BOW/>
